segments.s422-g73: همی before a verb is an older/poetic habitual/progressive marker meaning 'kept / continued to'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'. چنگش is 'his چنگ' where چنگ here means 'claw/grip, fingernail' (not the musical instrument); the possessive -ش attaches directly. به + اندرون here give a direction/placement 'toward/into the inside'—literally 'clutching inward'. Word order is Verb (همی گفت) then a noun phrase describing the simultaneous action.
segments.s422-g74: همی داشت uses داشت as an auxiliary giving a continuous/past-progressive sense 'kept holding / was keeping'. تا is the temporal conjunction 'until'. چهر او شد چو خون is a predicate: چهر 'face' + او 'his' + شد 'became' + چو 'like/as' + خون 'blood' — 'his face became like blood.' The sequence shows cause/result: he kept doing the action until his face assumed the appearance of blood.
segments.s422-g75: همان here means 'that same' or 'that very'. ناخنش is 'his nail' (ناخن + possessive -ش). پر ز + noun is a poetic pattern meaning 'full of [something]' (پر 'full (of)'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. خوناب is 'bloodwater / gore'. کرد is the simple past 'he made/caused' — together: 'he made that nail full of gore.'
segments.s422-g76: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a high-ranking general). بروها here is plural/collective (an older poetic use) referring to the men/warriors that go forth (literally 'those who go'); the text treats it as a group noun. پر از تاب کرد repeats the 'fill with' pattern (پر از = 'full of'), تاب here means 'brightness, radiance' (not endurance), so کرد again 'made/filled' — 'he filled the general and the warriors with radiance.'
segments.s422-g77: بخندید is past tense 'laughed' and with ازو (from/of him) means 'laughed at him' (a transitive sense). Word order is verb (بخندید) + prepositional object (ازو) + the subject name with adjective (فرخ اسفندیار). فرخ is an epithet 'fortunate/auspicious' attached before the name.
segments.s422-g78: چنین گفت is a formula 'thus said'. کای is the vocative particle 'O' (not the royal title here). The following رستم نامدار is the addressee 'Rostam, famed/renowned' with نامدار as an epithet. The clause introduces direct speech.
segments.s422-g79: تو امروز می‌خور is literal 'you eat/feast today' — می‌خور is the present habitual/continuous form used here to mean 'you feast/enjoy yourself'. که introduces the following temporal/result clause ('for / because / that'). فردا به رزم places the future time and direction: 'tomorrow into battle.'
segments.s422-g80: بپیچی is present/subjunctive of پیچیدن used here with future implication 'you will be turned/thrown into (battle)' or 'you will enter into battle' (the verb conveys movement/turning toward action). و links clauses. یادت نیاید is an impersonal/analytic construction 'your memory will not come' = 'you will not remember'; یادت is 'your memory' (literally 'your remembering') and نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive meaning 'does not come' (here future/irrealis sense). ز بزم means 'of/from the banquet' (i.e. you will not remember the feast).
segments.s422-g113: دهم is a first‑person action verb here (‘I give / I make’). بی‌نیازی is a noun meaning ‘freedom from need / self‑sufficiency’ and functions as the direct object (what is being given). سپاه ترا is a genitive/object cluster: سپاه 'army' + the enclitic ت (ترا) marking the second person object ‘you’; the full phrase means ‘your freedom from armies.’ The final را is the definite/object marker attached in classical/poetic word order.
segments.s422-g114: به چرخ اندر آرم is a verb phrase with its locative prepositional phrase first: به (towards/into) + چرخ (the sky/heaven, here a lofty place) + اندر (in/within) set the location, and آرم is the first‑person verb ‘I place / I set’ (poetic first‑person form). کلاه ترا is the object (kalaah = hat/crown) with the enclitic ت marking ‘you’ and را as the definite/object marker; overall: ‘I set your crown into the heavens.’
segments.s422-g115: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial (‘after that’); بیابم is a first‑person present/subjunctive/future‑oriented form of یابیدن used here as ‘I shall come / I shall reach / I shall find myself,’ and به نزدیک شاه is the direction/goal phrase where به introduces direction and نزدیک شاه means ‘near the king.’ Word order places the temporal phrase up front, then the verb and the directional phrase.
segments.s422-g116: This is a coordinated descriptive phrase modifying the approach: گرازان (boars) conjoined with adjectives خندان (smiling/laughing) and خرم (joyful), linked by و. The trailing prepositional phrase به راه ('on/along the road') gives the setting. In classical Persian adjectives commonly follow the noun they modify, as seen here.
segments.s422-g117: به مردی is an adverbial phrase modifying the action; here مردی is best read as 'manliness / as a man' (i.e., 'as befits a man'). ترا is the enclitic object 'you'. تاج بر سر نهم is the verb phrase where بر سر means 'upon the head' and نهم is the archaic/poetic first‑person present/subjunctive form of نهادن 'to put/place' ('I place'). Classical Persian often puts clitics (ترا) before the noun they relate to.
segments.s422-g118: سپاسی به گشتاسپ is a concessive/purpose phrase: سپاسی ('a thanks' or 'in thanks') + به گشتاسپ ('to/for Gushtasp'). زین بر نهم is parallel in structure to the previous clause: زین 'saddle' + بر 'on' + نهم (I put/place) — i.e. 'I will put a saddle on (him).' The noun سپاسی functions adverbially to mark the motive (‘as thanks’).
segments.s422-g119: ازان پس is again 'after that' (temporal). ببندم کمر is a verb phrase with ببندم (first‑person form of بستن/بستن‑derived sense 'to bind/tie/gird') meaning 'I will gird/tie (my) belt/loins' and کمر is the thing bound (here 'waist' or 'girdle'). بر میان is a locative expression ('around the middle') where بر indicates position on/around and میان = 'middle.' The clause expresses a planned action (classical present used for future/intent).
segments.s422-g120: چنانچون is the comparative 'just as / in the same way that.' ببستم is a first‑person past/perfect (poetic usage) of بندن/بستن meaning 'I bound/tied/girded' and functions as the model for the new action. به پیش کیان ('before the kings') places that past action in the kings' presence: به introduces the phrase and پیش = 'in front of / before / in the presence of', کیان = 'the kings (the Kayanids)'.
segments.s422-g121: همه روی پالیز is a noun phrase meaning “the whole face/surface of the orchard” (همه = whole; روی = face/surface; پالیز = orchard). بی‌خو is an adjectival phrase ‘without sleep / sleepless’ placed before the verb, and کنم is first-person singular present/subjunctive/volitional of کردن used here to express the speaker’s resolve (“I will / I shall make/keep …”).
segments.s422-g122: ز شادی is a prepositional/casual phrase ‘from/because of joy’ modifying the following verb. تن خوی(ش) را = “his/one’s own body” — here خوی + enclitic ش form خویش ‘one’s own’, and را is the definite/object marker marking تن خویش as the direct object. نو کنم repeats the first-person کنـم verb and means ‘I make (it) new / I renew (it)’, with کنـم again used volitionally.
segments.s422-g123: چو … باشی is a temporal/conditional clause: چو here means ‘when/if’ and باشی is second-person singular present/subjunctive of بودن used to state the condition ‘when you are king’. The conjunction و links the second coordinated clause; the verb for the second clause is omitted (elliptical), so و من پهلوان is understood as ‘and I [am] the champion’.
segments.s422-g124: This is a negative-existential/result clause: کسی را … نباشد روان literally ‘there will be no one who has spirit in the body’. کسی را with the object marker را topicalizes/emphasizes ‘no one’; به تن در is a locative phrase ‘in the body’ (به … در together indicate location/possession of the spirit), نباشد is negative present/subjunctive of بودن used here to mean ‘there will not be / there will be none’, and روان is the noun ‘soul/spirit’ serving as the predicate content.
segments.s422-g65: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. ناخن is the source noun 'nail'. فرو is an adverb/particle 'down' placed before the verb to show downward motion. ریختش is the past verb ریخت plus the enclitic -ش ('it/him/her') meaning 'it poured' or 'it spilled (on him/it)'; in classical Persian the object/clitic can attach to the verb. آب زرد (object phrase) = 'yellow water' (lit. water described by زرد) — the sentence orders the source phrase first (ز ناخن), then the directional adverb (فرو), then the verb with enclitic, and finally the noun phrase denoting what poured.
segments.s422-g66: همانا is a strong adverb 'verily/indeed'. نجنبید is the past-tense form with the negative prefix ن- meaning 'did not move/stir' (classical negative past). زان‌درد is the fused prepositional phrase ز + آن درد = 'from/because of that pain'. مرد is the noun 'man' (the one who did not move). The clause thus states cause and effect: 'indeed the man did not move because of that pain.'
segments.s422-g67: گرفت is the past tense 'took/seized'. آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time/then'. دست مهتر is the object phrase 'the (or the) chief's hand' (دست + noun). به دست here means 'into (his) hand' or 'with the hand' and together دست ... به دست expresses grasping the other person's hand with one's own. The subject is omitted (understood from context) as is common in Persian.
segments.s422-g68: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' introducing reported speech. گفت here functions as 'said' (past of گفتن) though the provided sense list is noun-ish; grammatically it is the 3rd-person past of 'to say' and links to the following vocative. کای is the vocative 'O' used to address someone. شاه یزدان‌پرست = 'pious/God-worshipping king' (شاه = king; یزدان‌پرست = God-worshipper/pious). The whole segment is the speech introduction: 'thus he said, "O pious king".'
segments.s422-g69: خنک here is an archaic/poetic felicitation ('blessed/fortunate'); this specific nuance is idiomatic in epic Persian and does not match the literal senses 'cool/refreshing' given. شاه گشتاسپ is the king's name 'King Gushtasp'. آن نامدار is a determiner + adjective 'that renowned (one)'. The adjective phrase praises the king; word order places the felicitation first, then the title and name, then the epithet.
segments.s422-g70: کجا here is rhetorical 'where/where is there' used to mean 'who/where would one find' (a common rhetorical question). پور دارد literally 'has a son' (پور = son; دارد = has/possesses, present). چو is the comparative 'like/as' and introduces the example (چو اسفندیار = 'like Esfandiyar'). Together the clause asks/remarks about having a son such as Esfandiyar.
segments.s422-g71: خنک آنک is an idiomatic opening 'blessed/fortunate is he who...' where آنک functions as a relative pronoun 'who/that'. چون تو modifies پسر as 'a son like you'. In the clause پسر زاید او the object (پسر) precedes the verb زاید ('gives birth/produces'); او is the (third-person) pronoun referring to the person who would have that son (subject). Classical Persian often places object before verb and then the pronoun subject after the verb.
segments.s422-g72: همی is an adverb/prefix giving iterative or emphatic sense ('indeed/still') and here helps form the future-oriented statement. فر گیتی is a compound noun phrase 'the world's royal glory' (فر = royal glory, گیتی = world). بیفزاید is the present/subjunctive-form with bi- prefix used in classical Persian to mean 'will add/increase' (future-oriented). او is the pronoun 'he' (subject). The clause therefore states that such a son 'will increase the world's glory.'
segments.s422-g97: به جای is the fixed prepositional phrase 'instead of' (literally 'to/for place'); می‌سرخ is a present/stative verb 'turns red / blushes' with no explicit subject (a common poetic omission); کین آوریم is a first-person plural verb phrase 'we bring/produce vengeance' where آوریم is a jussive/indicative plural form meaning 'we will/shall bring'. The clause reads: 'Instead of (blushing) we will bring vengeance.'
segments.s422-g98: This clause lists tactics: کمند 'a snare/lasso' is put as the thing brought; نبرد is the noun 'battle' (here contrasted with covert action); و is the coordinating 'and'; کمین 'ambush' is another noun; آوریم repeats the same first-person plural verb 'we will bring/bring about'. The line implies preference for snare/ambush over open battle: 'we will bring snares and lay ambushes (rather than fight openly)'.
segments.s422-g99: غو کوس خواهیم is a verbal phrase where غو 'tumult/uproar' qualifies کوس 'war-drum' and خواهیم ('we will / we desire') gives a future/optative sense: 'we will raise the drum's uproar'. از + آوای رود is a prepositional phrase 'from the voice/sound of the river' modifying how the uproar will be made (i.e. 'like/from the river's roar').
segments.s422-g100: به ... و به ... is a double dative/benefactive construction 'to/for the sword and to/for Gupal'; باشد درود uses باشَد in the optative/imperative sense 'let there be' plus the noun درود 'greeting/salutation', so the clause means 'let there be greetings to the sword and to Gupal.' گوپال here functions as a proper name/title.
segments.s422-g101: ببینی is a second-person present/subjunctive form 'you will see' (poetic future/imperative flavor). تو‌ای is a vocative 'you, O...' addressing Esfandiyar; فرخ is an epithet 'fortunate' placed before the proper name اسفندیار. Together: 'You, O fortunate Esfandiyar, will see...'.
segments.s422-g102: گراییدن is a verbal noun/infinitive here meaning 'the inclining/turning' and گردش کارزار is a genitive-like phrase 'the turning/circuit (گردش) of the battle (کارزار)'. The two nouns are linked by و 'and', giving 'the turning and the course of the battle.'
segments.s422-g103: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause; فردا 'tomorrow' is the time; بیایی is the second-person present/subjunctive 'you come' (with چو gives 'when you come'); به دشت نبرد is a prepositional phrase 'to the plain of battle' indicating destination. Together: 'When tomorrow you come to the plain of battle...'
segments.s422-g104: This line uses compact, repetitive poetic syntax. به آورد is a causative/iterative verbal form 'will bring forth / will cause to come'; مرد is 'a man' (here as an actor); اندر آید 'will come in / will enter' (intransitive, third-person); به مرد at the end marks direction/against whom 'to (or upon) a man'. The sense: 'one man will bring forth another, and a man will come upon a man' — i.e. men will be matched one against one. Note the inversion and repetition are typical of epic diction.
segments.s422-g105: ز باره (literally “from/at the occasion”) is a poetic phrase meaning “this time/on this occasion.” The verb بردارمت is the first‑person verb بردارم with the enclitic object ‑ت (“you”), here expressing intent/future: “I will take you (into)”; به آغوش is a prepositional phrase “into the embrace/arms” placed before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s422-g106: This clause gives the destination/source: ز میدان “from the field/arena (battlefield).” به نزدیک زال is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/near Zal.” The final verb آرمت is first person with the enclitic ‑ت (“you”); it is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning “I will place/bring (you) near,” so the verb governs the whole preceding prepositional phrase.
segments.s422-g107: نشانمت is a first‑person verb with enclitic object ‑ت (“you”); here it functions as an act of setting/seating or making someone a mark/insignia and so carries a future/intent sense (“I will set/seat you”). The following prepositional phrase بر نامور تخت عاج specifies the location “on the famed ivory throne” (بر + noun), with نامور qualifying تخت and عاج specifying the material.
segments.s422-g108: The clause begins with the (understood) verb نهم “I will place/put,” followed by two prepositional phrases: بر سرت “upon your head” and بر دل‌افروز تاج “a heart‑cheering crown.” The verb is first person and takes تاج as its object, while دل‌افروز is a compound adjective modifying تاج; word order places the object (trophy/crown) at the end for emphasis.
segments.s422-g109: کجا is an interrogative/adverb “where/how,” placed at the front for rhetorical effect. یافتستم is the past tense “I found/obtained,” with من as an emphatic pronoun; از کیقباد specifies the source “from Kay Qobad.” The clause thus asks rhetorically where (or how) the speaker could have gotten him from Kay Qobad.
segments.s422-g110: This is an optative/blessing: به مینو “to/in heaven,” همی adds continuing/everlasting force, جان او “his soul” is the subject, and باد شاد is the optative construction with باد (optative particle “may it be”) + predicate شاد “happy.” Together: “May his soul be forever happy in heaven.”
segments.s422-g111: گشایم is a first‑person verb (here expressing intent: “I will open”) governing در گنج “the treasure-house” (در + گنج). The coordinating و links to the next clause; هر خواسته “every desired thing/whatever you wish” is prepared as the direct object to be presented in the following clause.
segments.s422-g112: The (understood) verb نهم “I will place/put” starts the clause and takes پیش تو “before you/in your presence” as its locative, while یکسر آراسته is a compound adverb+adjective (یکسر “entirely/altogether” + آراسته “adorned/prepared”) modifying what is placed. The clause thus means “I will set/offer everything, wholly adorned, before you.”
segments.s422-g17: وزان پس که شد سوی هاماوران is a small temporal subordinate clause. وزان پس (literally “and from that/after that”) sets the time; که introduces the subordinate clause (“when/that”); شد is a past verb meaning here ‘came/turned’ or ‘happened’ in classical usage; سوی governs the following locative noun هاماوران (“toward the plains”). Word order is typical Persian: temporal phrase → conjunction → verbal clause → directional complement.
segments.s422-g18: ببستند پایش به بند گران is a full predicate: ببستند is the 3rd-person-plural past (‘they bound/tied’); پایش is the object ‘his foot’ (پای + possessive suffix -ش = his); به marks the relation 'to/in' (they bound his foot to/with); بند گران is the object phrase ‘heavy fetters’ with the adjective گران following the noun (typical Persian noun‑adjective order).
segments.s422-g19: ببردم ز ایرانیان لشکری is a clause with an archaic/epic verb form: ببردم is 1st-person past ‘I carried/led away’ (the b‑prefix occurs in older epic diction); ز introduces the source ‘from the Iranians’; لشکری is a noun meaning ‘a force/troops’ (here an army of Iranians). The word order is source phrase after the verb, common in poetry.
segments.s422-g20: به جایی که begins a locative relative: به جایی ‘to a place’ and که introduces the relative clause ‘where/that’. بد مهتری‌گر is a compound descriptive phrase: بد qualifies مهتری‌گر (‘one who seeks superiority/rank’) — a morally negative characterization — and سری here serves as a nominal head meaning ‘chief/head’ (poetic use). The whole phrase describes the sort of person or authority present at that place.
segments.s422-g21: بکشتم به جنگ اندرون شاهشان contains an archaic past verb form بکشتم (here functioning as ‘I killed’ or ‘I did kill’). به جنگ اندرون means ‘in the inner fighting/inner battle’ (به + جنگ + اندرون = in the battle within); شاهشان is ‘their king’ (شاه + possessive suffix -شان). The clause places the killing within the internal battle associated with their king.
segments.s422-g22: تهی کردم آن نامور گاهشان is a verb phrase built from the light verb کردن: تهی کردن ‘to empty’, here in the 1st-person past کردم ‘I made/did’ → ‘I emptied’. آن نامور گاهشان is the direct object: آن (demonstrative ‘that’) + نامور (‘renowned/famed’) + گاهشان (‘their seat/time/place’ with possessive -شان). The order Demonstrative–Adjective–Noun + possessive is typical poetic ordering.
segments.s422-g23: جهاندار کاوس کی بسته بود is a nominative phrase plus a relative/clausal comment. جهاندار (‘world‑ruler’, an honorific) apposes کاوس (the name Kay Kāvus). کی here is an archaic relative/pronominal particle ‘who’ (classical usage); بسته بود is the past passive/copular construction ‘was bound/tied’ (بسته = bound + بود = was).
segments.s422-g24: ز رنج و ز تیمار دل‌خسته بود is a predicate giving cause and state: ز introduces cause (‘from/through suffering’), رنج ‘suffering’ and تیمار ‘care/solicitude’ are parallel causes joined by و, دل‌خسته is the predicative adjective ‘heart‑weary’, and بود is the past copula ‘was’. The phrase literally says he was heart‑weary because of suffering and because of care.
segments.s422-g25: بیاوردم is a past-perfective 1st-person verb ('I brought' / 'I fetched'); here it introduces a transitive action. از بند is a prepositional phrase 'from the bond / from captivity' (بند = fetter/prison), and کاوس را is the definite direct object (کاوس = Kay Kavus) marked by را; the sequence is somewhat inverted for poetic effect but means 'I brought Kavus out of (his) bond'.
segments.s422-g26: This is a coordinated noun phrase naming the companions. همان is a demonstrative/identifying 'that same' / 'namely', the names follow joined by و ('and'), هم is an additive 'also', طوس is the hero Tous, and the final را again marks the whole list as definite/emphatic object — so the clause continues the object list of بیاوردم.
segments.s422-g27: به ایران is a directional prepositional phrase 'to/against Iran'. بد افراسیاب is a descriptive phrase 'the evil/bad Afrasiab' (بد as an epithet). آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time' placed after the name; together they mean 'the evil Afrasiab (came) against Iran at that time' (the verb is implied/elliptical in poetry).
segments.s422-g28: جهان پر ز درد is a compact construction: جهان 'the world' + پر 'full' + ز (poetic for از) 'of' + درد 'sorrow/affliction' → 'the world was full of pain'. از بد بدگمان literally 'from/ because of bad mistrust' (بد intensifies/characterizes بدگمان 'mistrust / suspicion'); the whole clause explains the cause of the world's affliction.
segments.s422-g29: به ایران is 'into/to Iran'. کشیدم is a past 1st-person verb 'I drew / I moved / I marched' (کشیدن used here in the sense 'to draw/advance (forces)'). ز هاماوران 'from the plains' gives the origin of the movement. Together: 'I advanced into Iran from the plains.'
segments.s422-g30: This coordinated NP is the entity moved or accompanying the movement: خود و شاه can be read as an intensive/identifying phrase ('the shah himself' or 'himself — the shah'); با لشکری بی‌کران is an adverbial/adjunct 'with an endless/boundless army' modifying the movement. In this line کشیدم takes as object or accompaniment 'the shah himself with an innumerable army.'
segments.s422-g31: شب تیره is a fronted temporal phrase 'a dark night'. تنها is an adverb/adjective 'alone/only'. برفتم is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'I departed / I went away' (here used for 'I left'), and ز پیش means 'from before / from their presence' (پیش = in front of / before, z = poetic variant of از). The whole segment: 'On a dark night I alone departed from before them.'
segments.s422-g32: همه نام جستم is a standard collocation 'I sought name/reputation from everyone' (نام جستن = to seek renown; جستم is 1st-person past 'I sought'). نه آرام خویش negates the alternative: نه = 'not', آرام خویش = 'my own rest/peace' (خویش here functions as a possessive 'one's own' combined with آرام). The clause means 'I sought fame from all, not my own peace.'
segments.s422-g57: بدو is a prepositional/adverbial clitic phrase meaning 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; گفت is the simple past 'said' (3rd person) following it. کای is an archaic vocative particle ('O'), and رستم پیلتن is the addressee: the name رستم followed by the place/epithet پیلتن (no overt ezāfe here because of the poetic register).
segments.s422-g58: چنانی که introduces a comparative/relative clause 'such as/so that', where که functions as the relativizer; بشنیدم is first person past 'I heard' reporting what was heard; از انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'from the assembly' (از + noun). The whole clause qualifies what was said.
segments.s422-g59: ستبرست is a predicative adjective with the copular sense 'is stout/firm' (archaic poetic form). بازوت is 'your arm' (بازو + possessive suffix -ت). The following simile introduced by چون ('like/as') applies to the noun ران شیر: 'like a lion's thigh' — the noun phrase is compacted in poetic order.
segments.s422-g60: برو here functions descriptively with یال (poetic/archaic usage meaning the mane/crest), giving the sense 'bristling/standing' (a verbal/adjectival use rather than the common imperative 'go'). چون introduces another simile 'like a(n) اژدهای دلیر' = 'like a brave dragon' modifying یال.
segments.s422-g61: میان here functions as 'mid(–), in the middle' (referring to the body/midsection) followed by the coordinated adjectives تنگ و باریک ('narrow and slim'). همچون introduces a simile 'like/as' and پلنگ is the simile's tenor ('a panther'), so the phrase describes the waist/midriff as panther‑like.
segments.s422-g62: به ویژه is an adverbial phrase 'especially'; کجا is an interrogative 'where' introducing the question clause. گرز is the noun 'mace/club' and گیرد is the 3rd person singular present/subjunctive of گرفتن here with the potential sense 'could seize / could get hold of'; به چنگ is a prepositional phrase 'into the hand/claw' (چنگ = grasp/claw). Together it asks rhetorically where a mace could find purchase in his (tight) grip.
segments.s422-g63: بیفشارد is a past tense verbal form meaning 'he squeezed/clenched' (poetic variant). چنگش is 'his claw/hand/grip' (چنگ + possessive -ش). میان سخن literally 'in the middle of the speech/words' — میان used adverbially here ('amid/among the words') and marks the time/setting of the action.
segments.s422-g64: ز is the poetic preposition 'because of/at' marking the target of the laugh; برنا 'the young (man)' is the object. بخندید is past 'laughed' (3rd person) and here takes the subject مرد کهن 'the old man' which follows the verb in typical poetic word order: 'the old man laughed (at) the young.'
segments.s422-g41: پدرم is the noun پدر with the possessive suffix -م 'my' (here 'my father'). آن دلیر گرانمایه مرد is an appositive noun phrase describing that father: آن (demonstrative 'that') precedes the adjectives دلیر ('brave') and گرانمایه ('esteemed/precious'), then the head noun مرد ('man/hero'). Word order is demonstrative → adjectives → noun, a typical poetic/Nastaʿlīq ordering.
segments.s422-g42: ز introduces the causal/ablative phrase ('from/because of'). ننگ اندران انجمن is a prepositional/genitive phrase—ننگ 'shame' modified by اندران ('therein/in that') and انجمن ('assembly'); خاک خورد is the verbal phrase 'ate dust' (خورد is the past of خوردن). Together the clause says he 'ate dust' (idiom: suffered humiliation) because of shame in that assembly. Note the simple past verb خورد refers back to the previously introduced subject (پدرم).
segments.s422-g43: که introduces a reported/subordinate clause ('that'). لهراسپ را is the marked direct object (را marks definiteness/emphasis). شاه is the predicate noun (i.e. 'king' — 'to call someone king'), بایست is a modal/obligative ('must/ought to'), and خواند is the verb 'to call/name' in a classical narrative form. The construction means 'that Lohrasp ought to be called king' (literal: 'one should call Lohrasp king').
segments.s422-g44: ازو is a genitive/ablative preform ('of him' / 'from him'), در جهان is a locative phrase ('in the world'), نام چندین is a noun phrase ('many a name' / 'many renownings'), and نماند is the past of ماندن ('remained'). The clause states that many names/fame of him remained in the world. Word order places the genitive/locative phrases before the noun and the verb نماند concludes the clause.
segments.s422-g45: چه is an exclamative ('what/how') introducing an exclamation. نازی is a poetic adjective expressing coquettishness/pride/vainglory in context. بدین here is the prepositional prefix ب- + this (با این → 'with this'); تاج گشتاسپی is the noun phrase 'the crown of Gushtasp' (گشتاسپی is the genitival/attributive form). The segment reads like an exclamatory comment on the crown of Gushtasp. Note archaic/poetic cliticization of 'با این' as بدین.
segments.s422-g46: بدین again functions as 'with this' (ب- + این). تازه آیین is a compound modifier meaning 'new ritual/ceremony' (تازه 'new' modifying آیین 'rite, custom'), and لهراسپی is an attributive form 'of Lohrasp.' The phrase comments on the novelty of Lohrasp's ritual tied to the crown; word order places the preposition and demonstrative before the modified noun phrase, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s422-g47: که introduces reported speech ('that / who'), گوید is the present/narrative 'he says' (poetic present used to report speech), followed by an imperative: برو ('go') and دست رستم ببند ('bind Rostam's hands') where دست رستم is the object possessed phrase and ببند is the imperative 'bind.' The sequence is a quoted command embedded in the narration; verb forms are imperative for the command.
segments.s422-g48: نبندد is a negative present/optative form (ن- prefix + بندد) meaning 'does not bind / will not bind / cannot bind' in this narrative; مرا is the object 'me' (here shown split as م + را), and دست چرخ بلند is the subject phrase 'the lofty hand of the wheel/sky' (چرخ بلند = the exalted heavenly hand/fate). The clause therefore says that the high hand of Fate/Heaven will not bind me. Note poetic word order: verb appears before its subject for emphasis.
segments.s422-g49: که‌گر is an archaic conditional particle (‘که گر’ = ‘if’). چرخ is the subject meaning the cosmic Wheel/fate; گوید is the present tense verbal form ‘says’ (poetic/older present). مراکاینِ نیوش is a poetic compound: مراکاین (the heavens/spheres) + نیوش (draught/potion) — here functioning as the thing Fate ‘speaks of’ or ‘offers’. Word order is conditional particle + subject + verb + complement.
segments.s422-g50: به introduces the instrument/target (‘to/with’); گرز گرانش is a noun phrase with گرز (mace) and the following گرانش qualifying it as ‘heavy/weighty’ (postpositive adjective). بمالم is the 1st-person present/jussive form (بـ- prefix + مالیدن) meaning ‘I will rub/strike’ in this context (here used of striking with a club). دو گوش is the object ‘both ears’ (literally ‘two ears’). The clause therefore expresses: ‘I will (with) my heavy mace strike both ears.’
segments.s422-g51: من is the explicit subject/emphatic pronoun. از کودکی is a temporal prepositional phrase ‘since/from childhood’. تا introduces the endpoint ‘until’; شدستم is an older past form ‘I became’ (پشت/archaic past), and کهن qualifies the result ‘old/aged’. The sequence gives a time span: ‘from childhood until I became old.’
segments.s422-g52: بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase ‘in this manner/like this’ (بدین = به + این). از کس marks the source/agent ‘from anyone’. نبردم here is the perfective/preterite negative ‘I did not take/receive’ — in context idiomatically ‘I have not heard/been told’ — and سخن is the noun ‘words/speech’. Word order is adverbial + source phrase + verb + object.
segments.s422-g53: مرا here indicates the experiencer/affected person (‘to me’ / ‘for me’) and is split in the list as م + را; خواری is the noun ‘humiliation/abasement’. از + پوزش و خواهش means ‘on account of/apart from apology and entreaty’ (pūzesh = apology, khāhesh = request/entreaty). است is the copula ‘is’. The clause literally reads ‘There is humiliation for me from apology and entreaty.’
segments.s422-g54: وزین in classical Persian is a contraction of و + زِ این = ‘and from this’ (here introducing the next cause/result). نرم گفتن is a verbal noun phrase ‘gentle/soft speaking’ (نرم = gently/soft, گفتن = speaking). مرا (again split as م + را in the list) marks the affected person ‘me’. کاهش است = ‘there is a diminution’ / ‘I am diminished’. Together: ‘And from this gentle speaking I am diminished.’
segments.s422-g55: ز is the poetic form of از meaning ‘from/because of’. تیزیش is تیز + -ش (possessive/nominalizing) = ‘its keenness/sharpness’. خندان شد is the compound verbal expression ‘became smiling’ (شد as auxiliary = ‘became’), so ‘Esfandiyar became smiling’ = ‘Esfandiyar smiled’. Proper name Esfandiyar is the subject.
segments.s422-g56: بیازید is a classical verb meaning ‘he opened (his mouth) / gaped / broke forth’ (here ‘opened [his mouth]’). و = ‘and’. دستش = دست + -ش is ‘his hand’. گرفت is past ‘took/seized’. استوار is an adjective ‘firm/steady’. Word order: verb (opening), conjunction, noun phrase (his hand) + verb (seized) + adjective (firm) describing how he took it.
segments.s422-g81: چو introduces a temporal/subordinate clause 'when'. The personal subject من follows it ('when I ...'). نهم is an archaic 1st-person present/subjunctive form 'I put/place' used here to state the speaker's action; زین زرین ('this golden saddle') is the direct object and بر سپاه is a locative/prepositional phrase 'upon the host/army'. Word order is poetic but follows Persian SOV tendencies with the verb near the end.
segments.s422-g82: به سر بر نهم is the continuation of the action: به سر marks the location 'on (the) head' and بر نهم repeats the same 1st-person verb 'I place/put on'. خسروانی is an adjectival form 'royal' modifying کلاه ('hat/helmet'); the whole phrase means 'I put the royal helmet on (my) head.' The verb form is the same archaic/subjunctive-use of نهم indicating the speaker's intent/act.
segments.s422-g83: This clause again uses the verb نهم ('I put/plant') understood at the end: به نیزه ... بر زمین = 'I plant (it) as a spear into the ground'. ز اسپت contains the possessive clitic -ت on اسپ ('your horse'), so ز اسپت = 'from/of your steed' (source or accompaniment). The prepositional بر زمین gives the place 'into/on the ground.' The phrasing is compressed and poetic (instrument/source expressed before the verb).
segments.s422-g84: ازان پس is a compound temporal phrase 'after that' (ازان = 'from that' + پس = 'after/then'). The pair نه ... نه is correlative negation 'neither ... nor', here negating two nouns: پرخاش جویی ('seeking quarrel/aggression' — a noun + جویی 'seeking/pursuit') and کین ('vengeance/hatred').
segments.s422-g85: دو دستت = 'your two hands' where -ت is the 2nd-person possessive clitic attached to دست. ببندم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I bind/tie' used here as a performative/future action; برم (from بردن) is 1st-person 'I will take/carry' and نزد شاه = 'before the king' ('نزد' = in the presence of). The clause outlines two coordinated actions (bind + bring) with the subject implied 'I'.
segments.s422-g86: بگویم is 1st-person subjunctive/hortative 'I shall say' used to introduce reported speech; که is the subordinator 'that'. The subordinate clause 'من زو ندیدم گناه' literally 'I did not see sin from him' — ز + و (here representing 'از او' = 'of/from him') marks 'in him', ندیدم is the negative past 'I did not see', and گناه is the object 'sin'. Note the mix of future/hortative main verb with a past-tense reported clause, a normal pattern in Persian reported speech.
segments.s422-g87: بباشیم is 1st-person plural subjunctive/hortative 'let us be' or 'we should be', giving a polite hortatory tone. پیشش = 'before him/in his presence' (پیش + clitic = پیشِ او). به خواهشگری uses به as an adverbial marker 'by/with' and خواهشگری is a noun 'entreaty/pleading', so the clause means 'let us appear before him pleading/entreating.'
segments.s422-g88: بسازیم is 1st-person plural subjunctive 'we shall make/arrange' from ساختن used here in the sense 'decide/settle'; هرگونه‌ای 'of any kind/in any manner' is an indefinite modifier, and داوری 'judgment' is the object. Together: 'we will arrange/accept any kind of judgment.' The verb is hortative/plural optative in tone ('let us arrange').
segments.s422-g89: رهانم is a verb form with the first‑person suffix -م: 'I free / I release'. The enclitic object pronoun ت (written separately here) plus the object marker را follow the verb, so the structure is Verb + clitic‑object + را + prepositional phrase: 'I free you (ت + را) from ...'. از introduces the source 'from', and the coordinated nouns غم، درد، رنج list kinds of sorrow/pain.
segments.s422-g90: بیابی is a second‑person form (optative/subjunctive) meaning 'may you find' or 'you may find' (an attested poetic wish). پس از = 'after' + noun: پس (here 'after') + از (source) + رنج 'suffering'. خوبی و گنج are the coordinated direct objects 'goodness and treasure' that بیابی governs; the subject 'you' is implied by the verb.
segments.s422-g91: بخندید here is a past (or perfect) 3rd‑person form 'he laughed' (poetic word order puts the verb first). The following رستم is the subject 'Rostam'. The preposition ز (poetic variant of از) introduces the person who is the object/target of the laughter: 'laughed at Esfandiyar.'
segments.s422-g92: بدو is the clitic phrase 'to him' (i.e., 'he said to him'). گفت is the past verb 'said' introducing direct speech. The quoted clause سیر آیی از کارزار is a rhetorical question: سیر ('satiation/being satisfied') + آیی (2nd‑person present/optative from آمدن, here idiomatically 'are you / have you become') = 'Are you satisfied?' with از کارزار 'with/from the battlefield' specifying what one might be satisfied of. Word order places the predicate before the complement in typical poetic style.
segments.s422-g93: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. دیده‌ای is 2nd‑person perfect/past ('have you seen' / 'did you see'), followed by its object رزم جنگاوران ('the battle/combat of the warriors'); Persian poetic word order places the verb before the object here.
segments.s422-g94: Again کجا 'where' introduces a question; یافتی is 2nd‑person past 'you found / did you find'. باد here is literal 'wind' or 'blast' (an image for the sweeping force of a mace), and گرز گران is a noun phrase 'mace (گرز) heavy (گران)' where the adjective follows the noun (poetic order): 'the blast of a heavy mace.' The clause asks rhetorically where one would encounter such a thing.
segments.s422-g95: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. بر is the preposition 'upon/toward' governing جزین ('جز این' = 'except this / other than this' in fused poetic form). روی 'face' is the object of بر, گردَد is the present/subjunctive 3rd‑person of گشتن 'to turn / become' meaning 'should turn', and سپهر 'the sky/heaven' is the subject placed after the verb by poetic inversion: 'if the sky should turn its face on any other.'
segments.s422-g96: بپوشید is a jussive/imperative form from پوشیدن here meaning 'let (it) cover / let (it) be covered' (i.e., 'let [love] spread/cover'). میان دو تن = 'between the two bodies/persons'. روی مهر = 'the face of مهر' where مهر means 'love/affection' and روی 'face' is its head noun; the phrase therefore means 'place/let the face of love be between the two bodies.'
segments.s422-g33: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'when'. دید is the simple past (3rd‑person) 'saw/beheld'. آن درفشان is the subject phrase 'that banner-waver/standard‑bearer' (درفشان as epithet/agent), and درفش مرا is the object 'my banner' (مرا here marks possession in this archaic/poetic order and is followed by the object marker را). Word order is topical/object then verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s422-g34: به گوش آمدش is a light‑verb construction meaning 'came to his ear' (آمدش = 'came to him' with the enclitic -ش = 'to him'). بانگ رخش مرا is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the cry/sound of my steed' placed before the clause; بانگ = sound/voice, رخش = steed, and again مرا/را mark possession/definiteness in poetic order.
segments.s422-g35: بپردخت is a past narrative verb meaning 'proceeded/passed' (classical/poetic use; prefix ب- + پرداخت). ایران is the destination/region, و links clauses, شد is the past/inchoative 'became/turned' and سوی چین means 'toward China' (سوی = toward, چین = China). The clause shows sequential movement in concise epic narration.
segments.s422-g36: جهان شد is 'the world became' (شد as copula/inchoative). پر از داد is a predicate 'full of justice' (پر = 'full (of)', combined with از + noun), and و پر آفرین repeats the pattern 'and full of praise/acclaim' (آفرین here = acclamation/praise). This is a nominative-predicative structure typical of classical Persian.
segments.s422-g37: گر is the conditional 'if'. از یال کاوس is a prepositional phrase 'from the mane of Kay Kavus' (یال = mane; کاوس = the name). خون آمدی uses آمدی in a hypothetical/contingent sense 'were to come' or 'came' in the protasis after گر (classical/poetic conditional form).
segments.s422-g38: ز پشتش = 'from his back' (ز = poetic 'from', پشت = back, -ش = 'his'). سیاوش is the subject name. چون آمدی here functions as an interrogative/relativizing phrase 'how did (he) come' or 'in what manner/when he came' (چون = 'how/as', آمدی = 'came'), giving a rhetorical question or contrast to the conditional protasis.
segments.s422-g39: وزو is a fused poetic connective meaning roughly 'and from him' (classical contraction of و + زو/زو), introducing the agent. شاه کیخسرو is the noun phrase 'the king Keykhosrow' followed by two epithets پاک و راد ('pure and generous'). The adjectives follow the name in typical epic style.
segments.s422-g40: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing the relative clause. لهراسپ را is the definite object 'Lohrasp' (را marks definiteness), تاج بر سر نهاد is a verb phrase 'placed the crown upon (his) head' (تاج = crown, بر = on/upon, سر = head, نهاد = past of نهادن 'placed'). The clause describes an action attributed to Keykhosrow.
segments.s422-g9: که is a relative 'who/that' introducing a clause. کندی is a poetic form expressing making slow/dulled (here used transitively: 'stun/dull'); the direct objects دل و مغز ('heart and brain') follow it without an overt object marker, a common poetic word order inversion. دیو سپید ('the White Demon') is the noun phrase modified by سپید 'white'.
segments.s422-g10: The second که introduces a further relative/casual clause 'who/for he'. دارد is the present/habitual third‑person singular 'has' or 'holds'. به بازوی خویش is a prepositional phrase 'in/on his own arm' where خوی + ش together form the possessive 'خویش' ('his own') with ش the enclitic pronoun. این امید is the object 'this hope' (the hope he relies on).
segments.s422-g11: سر جادوان را is the definite direct object 'the heads of the enchanters' (سر + genitive جادوان, plus object marker را). بکندم is the 1st‑person past/epic past of کندن with the prefixed ب‑, yielding 'I tore off' or 'I removed'. ز تن is the ablative 'from the body', completing the transitive action.
segments.s422-g12: ستودان is the noun 'praise' used as an object (poetically placed before the verb). ندیدند is the negative past plural 'they did not see'. The following و‑items enumerate the things not seen: گور 'grave' and کفن 'shroud'. The clause therefore states a negative perception: 'they saw neither praise nor grave nor shroud.'
segments.s422-g13: ز بند گران is an ablative phrase 'from the heavy bonds/chains' (ز + noun + adjective). بردمش is the verb 'they/ I carried him' with the object‑clitic ‑مش ('him/it') attached to بردن. سوی تخت is the destination 'toward the throne' (سوی = toward, تخت = throne). Poetic order places adverbials before or around the verb.
segments.s422-g14: شد is the past 'became' or 'happened'. ایران بدو شاد is an inverted (poetic) order meaning 'Iran became glad for him' (ایران subject, بدو = برای او / to him, شاد = glad). The verb 'شد' supplies the predicate; او نیکبخت is a short clause 'and he (was) fortunate' where the copula is omitted (understood from شد).
segments.s422-g15: مرا (here split in the word list as م + را) is the dative/topic form 'for me' (equivalent to 'I had' in existential constructions). یار رخش بود = 'there was the companion Rakhsh' (رخش, the name of the steed, placed before بود). در هفتخوان is an adverbial 'in the Seven Trials' (هفتخوان = the sevenfold ordeals). The whole clause means 'for me there was Rakhsh as a companion in the Seven Trials.'
segments.s422-g16: که introduces an explanatory clause. شمشیر تیزم is 'my sharp sword' (تیز + enclitic ‑م = 'my', modifying شمشیر). جهان‌بخش is a compound adjective 'world‑bestowing' or 'world‑giving' used predicatively; بود is the copula 'was.' The clause states that the speaker's keen sword was famed as world‑bestowing.
segments.s422-g1: چنین (thus) is a fronted adverb; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' placed before the subject for poetic word order; رستم is the subject; به marks the indirect object and اسفندیار is the addressee. The whole segment = 'Thus Rostam said to Esfandiyar.'
segments.s422-g2: که introduces reported speech ('that'); کردار is the noun 'deed/action'; ماند (from ماندن) here has the sense of 'last/ endure' — a simple/presentive perfective in classical style meaning 'will remain' or 'endure'; ز ('from') is the poetic preposition meaning 'of/from us'; ما is the first‑person plural pronoun; یادگار is the noun 'memorial/remembrance'.
segments.s422-g3: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'. داد is used as the noun 'justice' (so داد + باش = 'be just' — باش is the imperative 'be'), and و بشنو coordinates a second imperative 'and listen'; سخن is the direct object 'speech/words'.
segments.s422-g4: ازین is از + این ('from/of this'); نامبردار is an epithet meaning 'named/renowned' (here 'renowned'); مرد is 'man' used in the epic sense 'hero/valiant man'; کهن is an adjective 'ancient/old'. The phrase = 'of this renowned old man.'
segments.s422-g5: اگر is the conditional 'if'. من is the first‑person pronoun 'I'. نرفتی is a poetical/archaic verb form: though it looks like a second‑person past, in epic usage it functions as a negative/subjunctive sense 'should not go / do not go' referring to the speaker — here 'if I do not go'. به + مازندران marks the destination 'to Mazandaran.'
segments.s422-g6: به گردن is a prepositional phrase 'upon the neck' (i.e., 'on your shoulders / upon you'); برآورده is a verbal/adjectival form from برآوردن meaning 'lifted/put on' (here an instruction: 'set / take up'); گرز گران is a noun phrase 'the heavy mace' with گران as adjective 'heavy/weighty'. The whole = 'take the heavy mace upon your neck.'
segments.s422-g7: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. بسته is the past participle 'bound/tied' (passive); بد is an adverbial use 'badly/ill' modifying بسته; گیو و کاوس و طوس are a coordinated list of proper names (Giv, Kay Kavus, Tous) serving as the patients of 'bound'. Word order is poetic but straightforward: 'Where have Giv, Kay Kavus, and Tous been badly bound?'
segments.s422-g8: شده is the perfect/passive auxiliary 'have become' (resultative). گوش کر is the adjectival phrase 'ear-deaf' = 'deaf'; یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/utterly' modifying the state; از introduces the cause 'from', بانگ is 'the sound/cry' and کوس is the 'war-drum' that produces it. Together: 'their ears have become entirely deaf from the sound of the drum.'

sentences.s422-s37: He kept saying it and kept clutching inward with his hand until his face turned like blood.
sentences.s422-s38: He filled that very nail with gore; he made the spahbed and the warriors brim with radiance.
sentences.s422-s39: Fortunate Esfandiyar laughed at him and thus said, 'O renowned Rostam...'
sentences.s422-s40: You feast today; tomorrow you'll be in battle and will not remember the banquet.
sentences.s422-s57: I will make you independent of armies and place your crown in the heavens.
sentences.s422-s58: After that I will come near the king, with boars merry and laughing along the road.
sentences.s422-s59: I will place a crown on your head as a man; in gratitude to Gushtasp I will put a saddle on (him).
sentences.s422-s60: After that I will gird my loins about my middle, just as I girded them before the kings.
sentences.s422-s61: I will lie sleepless across the whole orchard; out of joy I will make my body new.
sentences.s422-s62: When you are king and I the champion, no soul will remain in anyone’s body.
sentences.s422-s33: Yellow water poured down from the nail; indeed the man did not move from that pain.
sentences.s422-s34: At that time he took the chief's hand in his hand and thus said, 'O pious king.'
sentences.s422-s35: Blessed is that famed King Gushtasp who has a son like Esfandiyar.
sentences.s422-s36: Blessed is he to whom a son like you is born; he will indeed increase the world's glory.
sentences.s422-s49: Instead of (your) blushing we will bring forth hatred; we will set snares, not open battle, and we will lay ambushes.
sentences.s422-s50: We will make the drum's uproar from the river's sound; salutations be to the sword and to Gupal.
sentences.s422-s51: You shall see, O fortunate Esfandiyar, the turning and the course of the battle.
sentences.s422-s52: When tomorrow you come to the plain of battle, one man will bring forth another — man shall meet man.
sentences.s422-s53: This time I will take you in my arms; from the battlefield I will bring you near to Zal.
sentences.s422-s54: I will seat you upon the famed ivory throne; I will place a heart‑delighting crown upon your head.
sentences.s422-s55: How could I have obtained him from Kay Qobad? May his soul be ever happy in heaven.
sentences.s422-s56: I will open the treasure-house, and I will set every desired thing before you, wholly adorned.
sentences.s422-s9: And from that time, when he turned toward the level plains, they bound his foot in heavy fetters.
sentences.s422-s10: I led an Iranian force away to a place where a wicked seeker of rank held sway.
sentences.s422-s11: I slew (them) in the inner fighting of their king; I emptied that famed seat/time of theirs.
sentences.s422-s12: The world‑ruler Kay Kāvus, who was bound, was heart‑weary from suffering and from solicitude.
sentences.s422-s13: I brought Kavus out of his bonds — and likewise Giv and Goudarz and also Tus.
sentences.s422-s14: The evil Afrasiab came against Iran; at that time the world was full of pain from wicked mistrust.
sentences.s422-s15: I marched into Iran from the plains, bringing (with me) the shah himself with an endless army.
sentences.s422-s16: On a dark night I slipped away alone from before them; I sought renown from everyone, not my own rest.
sentences.s422-s29: He said to him, 'O Rostam of Piltan, such as I heard from the assembly.'
sentences.s422-s30: Your arm is stout like a lion's thigh, and your mane bristles like a brave dragon's.
sentences.s422-s31: Your midsection is narrow and slim like a panther's; especially, where could a mace be taken in (i.e. get purchase by) your grasp?
sentences.s422-s32: He clenched his hand amid the words; the old man laughed at the young man.
sentences.s422-s21: My father, that brave and noble man, from shame in that assembly ate dust.
sentences.s422-s22: That Lohrasp should be called king — many a name of his remained in the world.
sentences.s422-s23: What a vanity with this crown of Gushtasp, with this new ritual of Lohrasp!
sentences.s422-s24: He says, 'Go, bind Rostam's hands'; the high hand of the heavens will not bind me.
sentences.s422-s25: If the Wheel (fate) tells me of this heavenly draught, I will strike both ears with my heavy mace.
sentences.s422-s26: From my childhood until I grew old, I have not heard such words from anyone.
sentences.s422-s27: Humiliation comes to me from apologies and entreaties; and from such soft speaking I am diminished.
sentences.s422-s28: From its keenness Esfandiyar smiled; he opened his mouth and firmly took his hand.
sentences.s422-s41: When I place this golden saddle upon the host and put the royal helmet on my head.
sentences.s422-s42: I will plant (my) spear from your horse in the ground; after that, neither quarrel-seeking nor vengeance.
sentences.s422-s43: I will bind your two hands and take you before the king; I will say that I saw no sin in him.
sentences.s422-s44: Let us stand before him in entreaty and arrange any kind of judgment.
sentences.s422-s45: I will free you from sorrow, pain, and suffering; may you, after hardship, find goodness and treasure.
sentences.s422-s46: Rostam laughed at Esfandiyar; he said to him, 'Have you had enough of the battlefield?'
sentences.s422-s47: Where have you seen the warriors' combat? Where did you ever find the gust (the blast) of a heavy mace?
sentences.s422-s48: If the sky should turn its face toward any but this, let love's face be spread between the two bodies.
sentences.s422-s17: When that standard-bearer saw my banner, the sound of my steed reached his ear.
sentences.s422-s18: He passed through Iran and turned toward China; the world became full of justice and full of acclaim.
sentences.s422-s19: If blood came from Kay Kavus's mane, how did Siyâvash come from his back?
sentences.s422-s20: And from him the king Keykhosrow, pure and generous, — who placed the crown upon Lohrasp's head.
sentences.s422-s5: who stunned the heart and brain of the White Demon, who placed his hope in his own arm.
sentences.s422-s6: I tore the magicians’ heads from their bodies; they saw neither praise nor grave nor shroud.
sentences.s422-s7: They led him from his heavy bonds to the throne; Iran rejoiced for him, and he was fortunate.
sentences.s422-s8: I had Rakhsh as a companion in the Seven Trials; my keen sword was world-bestowing.
sentences.s422-s1: Rostam thus said to Esfandiyar: "Deeds will remain as our memorial."
sentences.s422-s2: Now be just, and listen to the words of this renowned old man.
sentences.s422-s3: If I do not go to Mazandaran, then take upon your neck the heavy mace.
sentences.s422-s4: Where have Giv, Kay Kavus, and Tous been badly bound? Their ears have become entirely deaf from the sound of the drum.
